transcript:
In my experience, the conversations around Metformin have shifted dramatically over the last five years. What started as a standard diabetes medication has somehow become the cornerstone of every longevity protocol on the internet. You have tech executives in Silicon Valley taking it purely for anti-aging purposes. You have biohackers combining it with growth hormone and fasting protocols. And perhaps most concerningly, you have healthy individuals in their thirties and forties starting this drug because they watched a podcast or read an article suggesting it will help them live longer. 

So, what I'm going to do in this video today is walk you through the things that almost nobody discusses when they hand out this prescription. We're talking about the exercise paradox, the B12 deficiency that creeps up on you, the mitochondrial impacts, and why for some people, this drug might actually be working against their longevity goals rather than for them. And then at the end, I'll give you the exact framework that I use to assess whether someone actually needs this medication versus the lifestyle interventions that consistently outperform it in the research. 

If you're new to the channel, then hello. I'm Dr. Alex. I'm an emergency medicine doctor with an obsession about the things that make us live longer and what truly prevents the diseases that we see every day in the emergency department. Now, I've seen the consequences of long-term Metformin use that weren't properly monitored. I've seen the B12 deficiencies that present with irreversible neurological symptoms. And I've also seen the hype around this drug reach levels that the current evidence simply doesn't support for healthy individuals. 

What you'll notice here is that I'm not going to give you a simple yes or no answer. The reality is more nuanced than that. But what I will say upfront is that if you are taking Metformin without regular monitoring of specific biomarkers, or if you're taking it without understanding how it interacts with your exercise routine, then you are flying blind in a way that could have serious consequences years down the line. 

Now, the first thing we need to address, and this one seems to get lost in all the longevity discussions, is that Metformin is fundamentally a mitochondrial inhibitor. Stay with me because this is actually one of the most important biochemical realities of this drug that almost nobody discusses. Metformin works primarily by inhibiting complex I of the mitochondrial electron transport chain. This reduces ATP production, which activates AMPK, which then suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis. That is how it lowers blood sugar. 

But here's the problem. Your mitochondria are not just energy factories. They are signaling hubs that coordinate adaptation to stress. And exercise is a stressor. When you exercise, you create reactive oxygen species and energy depletion that signals your muscles to adapt, to build new mitochondria, to become more efficient. This process is called mitochondrial biogenesis. 

What I found in my review of the literature is that Metformin appears to blunt this adaptation. There was a landmark study published in Aging Cell in 2019 that randomized healthy older adults to either Metformin or placebo while undertaking a 12-week resistance training program. The results were genuinely surprising to the longevity community. The Metformin group saw significantly smaller improvements in insulin sensitivity, mitochondrial respiration, and muscle hypertrophy compared to the placebo group. Essentially, the drug canceled out a meaningful portion of the exercise benefit. 

Now, the reason this happens is that Metformin and exercise both activate AMPK, but through different mechanisms. When you combine them, you get a ceiling effect or even a interference pattern where the drug prevents the exercise-induced signaling that builds new mitochondria. And the flip side is really telling. If you're taking Metformin specifically for longevity but you're also training regularly, you might be paying for a medication that is actively working against your primary intervention. 

This isn't just one study. There was follow-up research in Diabetes Care that found similar results in prediabetic patients doing aerobic training. The Metformin group had attenuated improvements in VO2 max and mitochondrial function. So the consistency here matters enormously. If you are taking this drug and exercising regularly, you need to understand that you are running an uncontrolled experiment on your own physiology. 

Moving on to point number two then. So the first thing that I check in anyone who comes to me having been on Metformin for more than six months is their B12 status. And I cannot stress enough how often this gets missed by primary care physicians who are juggling ten-minute appointments. 

Metformin causes vitamin B12 malabsorption through multiple mechanisms. It affects the calcium-dependent intrinsic factor-vitamin B12 receptor in the ileum. It alters gut motility. It changes the gut microbiome in ways that reduce B12 production and absorption. And this isn't a rare side effect. Large studies suggest that between 10% and 30% of long-term Metformin users develop B12 deficiency, and the longer you take it, the higher the risk becomes. 

Now, the big question on everybody's lips is why does this matter so much? Well, vitamin B12 is absolutely critical for neurological function. It maintains the myelin sheaths around your nerves. When levels drop, you get peripheral neuropathy, cognitive decline, mood disturbances, and in severe cases, irreversible spinal cord damage. 

Here's the insidious part. The symptoms of B12 deficiency develop slowly, over months or years. Early signs include fatigue, brain fog, tingling in the hands and feet, and balance problems. These symptoms are often attributed to aging or to other conditions, so the deficiency goes undiagnosed. I've seen patients in the emergency department presenting with ataxia and severe anemia who had been on Metformin for five years without a single B12 check. 

For longevity, B12 status is non-negotiable. It is required for methylation processes that regulate DNA expression, homocysteine metabolism, and cardiovascular health. Elevated homocysteine from B12 deficiency is an independent risk factor for stroke, heart disease, and dementia. There was a study in the Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism that found Metformin-associated B12 deficiency increased the risk of cognitive decline by nearly double over five years in diabetic patients. 

So if you are taking Metformin, you need to be checking your B12 levels annually at absolute minimum, preferably every six months. And here's the crucial point that most people miss: the standard serum B12 test can miss functional deficiency. You should also be checking methylmalonic acid or homocysteine levels to get a true picture of your B12 status. If these are elevated, you need supplementation regardless of your serum B12 number. 

The next thing I want to discuss is the gut microbiome impact, because this is where the biology gets really interesting. Metformin is known to cause gastrointestinal side effects in up to 30% of users. Diarrhea, nausea, abdominal discomfort. But beyond these acute symptoms, the drug fundamentally alters the composition of your gut bacteria. 

Research published in Nature showed that Metformin increases the abundance of Akkermansia muciniphila, a bacterium associated with improved metabolic health. This is often cited as a benefit of the drug. However, it also reduces populations of other beneficial species involved in short-chain fatty acid production, bile acid metabolism, and yes, B12 synthesis. 

Your gut microbiome is not just a digestive aid. It trains your immune system, produces neurotransmitters, metabolizes hormones, and regulates inflammation throughout your body. Chronic disruption of this ecosystem has been linked to autoimmune conditions, depression, inflammatory bowel disease, and metabolic syndrome. When you take Metformin daily for years, you are applying a selective pressure on this complex ecosystem that we do not fully understand. 

What's particularly concerning is that some of the purported benefits of Metformin might actually be due to changes in the microbiome that could potentially be achieved through diet and fermented foods without the systemic side effects. The Akkermansia increases seen with Metformin are also seen with prebiotic fibers, polyphenol-rich foods like extra virgin olive oil, and caloric restriction. So are we taking a drug with significant side effects to achieve something that lifestyle interventions could accomplish? 

Now let's talk about testosterone, particularly for the men watching this. There is consistent evidence that Metformin lowers testosterone levels in men, particularly those with diabetes or obesity. A meta-analysis in the Journal of Clinical Medicine found that Metformin significantly reduced total and free testosterone compared to placebo or other diabetes treatments. 

For longevity, this matters enormously. Testosterone is critical for maintaining muscle mass, bone density, cognitive function, mood, and cardiovascular health. The age-related decline in testosterone, often called andropause, is associated with frailty, sarcopenia, and increased mortality. If you are taking a medication that accelerates this decline in your thirties or forties in the name of longevity, you are potentially trading one risk factor for another. 

The mechanism appears to be related to suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and direct effects on Leydig cells in the testes. For men experiencing fatigue, low libido, or difficulty maintaining muscle mass while on Metformin, this is a conversation that needs to happen with your doctor. Yet almost nobody mentions this when prescribing the drug for off-label longevity use. 

Okay, so moving on to the lactic acidosis risk. Now, this is rare, but it is the reason Metformin carries a black box warning and why it is contraindicated in certain conditions. Metformin inhibits hepatic lactate uptake and gluconeogenesis from lactate. Under normal circumstances, this is fine. But if you have reduced kidney function, severe infection, hypoxia, or dehydration, lactate can accumulate to dangerous levels causing metabolic acidosis. 

In the emergency department, I have seen cases of Metformin-associated lactic acidosis. It is a life-threatening condition with a mortality rate exceeding 50% in severe cases. The risk increases with age, renal impairment, liver disease, and heart failure. If you are taking Metformin for longevity and you develop a severe illness, you need to stop the drug immediately and seek medical attention. But how many people taking this for anti-aging purposes know this? How many have clear instructions to hold the medication during acute illness? 

The other issue is contrast dye. If you need a CT scan with contrast for any reason, Metformin needs to be stopped 48 hours before and after the procedure due to the risk of acute kidney injury precipitating lactic acidosis. This is practical information that could save your life, yet it is rarely discussed in the context of preventative use. 

Now, let's address the elephant in the room. The TAME trial. The Targeting Aging with Metformin study has been discussed for years as the definitive trial that will prove whether Metformin works for longevity in humans. But here's what you need to understand. This trial is looking at whether Metformin can delay the onset of age-related diseases in people who already have one age-related condition. It is not studying healthy individuals. It is not a longevity trial in the sense that most people think. It is a comorbidity delay trial. 

The evidence for Metformin extending lifespan in humans does not exist. The evidence in mice and worms is compelling, yes. But humans are not mice. We have different metabolic regulation, different cancer biology, different repair mechanisms. The ITP (Interventions Testing Program) in mice showed Metformin did not extend lifespan in genetically heterogeneous mice when started at nine months, though it did show some benefit when started earlier. But mouse studies fail to translate to humans more often than not. 

What we do have is the UKPDS study showing reduced cardiovascular events in diabetics taking Metformin. But these are diabetics. Their baseline risk is different. You cannot extrapolate that benefit to someone with normal glucose regulation and assume the risk-benefit ratio remains favorable. 

So, what should you do if you are currently taking Metformin? First, get your B12, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine checked immediately if you haven't in the last six months. Second, consider the exercise interference. If you are training regularly, particularly for mitochondrial adaptation and muscle growth, you may want to discuss timing the dose away from your workouts or reconsidering the need for the drug altogether. 

Third, understand your kidney function. Get an eGFR and creatinine checked regularly. If your kidney function declines, the drug needs to stop. Fourth, have a plan for acute illness. Know to stop the drug if you become dehydrated, develop severe vomiting or diarrhea, or require imaging with contrast. 

Fifth, monitor your testosterone if you are male, and consider whether the metabolic benefits you are seeing could be achieved through other means. 

Now, the obvious question that many of you will have at this point is what should I do instead? If Metformin has all these potential downsides, what are the alternatives for improving insulin sensitivity and promoting longevity? 

The answer is that the most potent AMPK activator and longevity intervention we know of is not a drug. It is exercise, particularly zone 2 aerobic training and resistance training. Exercise activates AMPK without blunting mitochondrial biogenesis. It improves insulin sensitivity through multiple pathways. It builds muscle, which is the primary sink for glucose disposal. It stimulates BDNF for brain health. The list goes on. 

The second intervention is time-restricted eating or intermittent fasting. These create the energy depletion signals that activate AMPK and autophagy naturally. A 2019 study in the New England Journal of Medicine reviewed the evidence for intermittent fasting and found comparable or superior metabolic benefits to Metformin in prediabetic populations, without the side effect profile. 

The third is berberine, a plant alkaloid that activates AMPK through similar pathways to Metformin but with a different side effect profile. Studies have shown comparable glucose-lowering effects to Metformin in some trials, though the evidence base is smaller. However, it also has issues with CYP450 interactions and gastrointestinal side effects, so it is not without risk. 

The fourth is optimizing your body composition. Visceral adipose tissue is the primary driver of insulin resistance. Losing excess weight, particularly around the midsection, improves metabolic health more reliably than any pharmaceutical intervention. 

The fifth is sleep optimization. Poor sleep disrupts glucose regulation and increases insulin resistance immediately. One night of sleep deprivation can induce a prediabetic glucose profile in healthy individuals. Fixing your sleep is free and has no side effects. 

The sixth is building muscle through resistance training. Muscle tissue is your glucose reservoir. The more you have, the better your insulin sensitivity. This is why grip strength and leg strength are such powerful predictors of mortality and diabetes risk. 

Now, let me be clear about when Metformin is absolutely indicated. If you have type 2 diabetes, particularly if you cannot achieve glycemic control through lifestyle alone, Metformin is first-line therapy for good reason. It reduces HbA1c, it has cardiovascular benefits in this population, and it is generally well-tolerated. If you have polycystic ovary syndrome, it can be helpful for metabolic and fertility issues. If you are prediabetic with significant metabolic dysfunction and lifestyle interventions have failed, it is a reasonable consideration. 

But for the healthy individual looking to live longer? The risk-benefit calculation changes. You are taking a drug that inhibits mitochondrial function, lowers testosterone, depletes B12, disrupts gut flora, and potentially blunts the benefits of exercise, all to treat a condition you do not have. 

There was a large observational study published in the Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism that looked at over 78,000 individuals. It found that while Metformin reduced cancer risk in diabetics compared to other treatments, when compared to healthy controls, the cancer risk was actually higher. This suggests that the diabetes itself is the risk factor, not the lack of Metformin, and that Metformin is simply treating the diabetes rather than providing a universal protective effect. 

Another study in Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism followed over 12,000 matched pairs of diabetic and non-diabetic individuals. It found that while Metformin reduced mortality risk compared to other diabetes medications, it did not reduce mortality to the level of healthy non-diabetic individuals. In other words, it treats the disease but does not recreate the physiology of health. 

So, what is my personal stance? I do not take Metformin. Despite having access to it and understanding the theoretical mechanisms, I prioritize the interventions that have clear benefit-to-risk ratios in healthy populations. I focus on zone 2 training five hours per week, resistance training three times per week, time-restricted eating with a 16:8 window, optimal sleep, and body composition management. These interventions have robust evidence for longevity without the pharmaceutical side effects. 

If I were prediabetic or diabetic, I would consider Metformin, but only with strict monitoring protocols. I would check B12 every six months. I would time the dose away from exercise. I would monitor kidney function quarterly. And I would have a clear exit strategy to discontinue the drug if lifestyle interventions succeeded in normalizing my metabolic markers. 

The tragedy of the current longevity scene is that people want a pill to solve problems that require behavioral change. Metformin is attractive because it is easier than training consistently and eating well. But the evidence suggests that the lifestyle interventions work better, particularly when you factor in the side effects and monitoring requirements of the drug. 

Let me just make a really specific point here that I sort of touched on in the introduction because I know some people will be expecting me to recommend a stack that includes Metformin alongside various supplements. I do not take a supplement stack that includes Metformin, and I'm not going to suggest that healthy individuals should either. The evidence base for Metformin in longevity for healthy people is theoretical and based on animal data. The human data shows risks that are not adequately discussed. 

Every advert that you see from an influencer promoting Metformin as a universal longevity drug without discussing B12, exercise interference, or lactic acidosis risk is giving you incomplete information. And all of this creates a big problem because it gives people the impression that they can outpharmaceutical a poor lifestyle. Well, they cannot. 

The populations around the world who live the longest, the Blue Zones, do not take Metformin. What they do is eat real food, move regularly throughout the day, maintain strong social connections, and have a sense of purpose. Their insulin sensitivity comes from activity and plant-rich diets, not from mitochondrial inhibitors. 

There was a huge meta-analysis that took data from multiple randomized control trials looking at Metformin versus lifestyle intervention in prediabetes. The lifestyle intervention group had a 58% reduction in diabetes incidence compared to placebo, while Metformin had a 31% reduction. And the lifestyle benefits persisted for years after the intervention ended, while the Metformin benefit stopped when the drug stopped. 

Now, a lot of you may be wondering how to practically implement this if you are currently on Metformin and concerned about what I've discussed today. The honest answer is to have a structured conversation with your prescribing physician. Bring the studies on B12 deficiency. Discuss the exercise interference data. Ask about monitoring protocols. If they cannot provide satisfactory answers, seek an endocrinologist who understands these nuances. 

Do not stop the drug abruptly if you are diabetic, as this could cause dangerous blood sugar spikes. But do not continue taking it blindly without understanding the long-term risks to your nervous system, your hormones, and your exercise adaptations. 

Right, then let me just pull all of this together and give you the framework that I use when evaluating whether Metformin is appropriate for an individual. 

First, assess metabolic status. Check fasting insulin, fasting glucose, HbA1c, and ideally an oral glucose tolerance test with insulin levels. If these are normal, the indication for Metformin is weak. 

Second, assess kidney function. Check eGFR and creatinine. If eGFR is below 45, Metformin is contraindicated. If between 45 and 60, use with caution and reduced dosing. 

Third, assess B12 status. Check serum B12, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine before starting and every six months thereafter. 

Fourth, assess exercise capacity and goals. If you are an athlete or serious about building muscle and mitochondrial capacity, consider whether the drug aligns with your goals. 

Fifth, assess lifestyle foundation. If you are not already doing zone 2 training, resistance training, time-restricted eating, and sleep optimization, address these first. They have better evidence and fewer side effects. 

Sixth, if medication is still indicated after all this, consider starting with the lowest effective dose, typically 500mg daily with food, and titrating up slowly based on tolerance and glucose monitoring. 

Seventh, establish a holding protocol for acute illness. Know when to stop the drug for dehydration, infection, or procedures requiring contrast. 

Eighth, schedule regular follow-ups every three to six months for kidney function and B12 monitoring. 

When you write it out like that, it looks quite involved, and it is. Metformin is not the benign supplement that social media often portrays it as. It is a pharmaceutical with significant biological effects that require monitoring. 

The common theme running through all of these videos on this channel is that small things done consistently and improved over time is where the real health gains come from. Metformin is not a small thing. It is a systemic intervention that affects energy production at the cellular level. Treat it with the respect that deserves. 

If you are taking Metformin now, get your B12 checked this week. If you are considering starting it, ask yourself whether you have optimized the lifestyle interventions first. Because in my experience, the morning routine, the training schedule, the sleep hygiene, and the diet will move your longevity markers more reliably than any pill ever will. And they come with side effects like better mood, stronger muscles, and clearer cognition rather than diarrhea and neuropathy. 

The choice, as always, is yours. But make it an informed one.
topic: The hidden risks and untold mechanisms of Metformin use for longevity, including B12 deficiency, exercise interference, mitochondrial impacts, and evidence-based alternatives
title: Taking Metformin? You Were Never Told This...
